story:
Coda hovers his hand over a pulsating green orb, exhausted and, he admits, bored. What began as science for the good of humanity has curdled into a race. His holographic advisor—Stephen Hawking’s projection—flickers into focus to remind him that competition has always haunted discovery. Coda blames Malik for breaking from the team and going rogue. Hawking, weary, urges him to see beyond ego.

Across the city, Malik makes the same case to his own spectral mentor, Albert Einstein. Malik insists he won’t be erased from history; he wants the credit he deserves. Einstein calls the rivalry juvenile and, with biting irony, suggests a crude solution—kill Coda’s father. Malik rejects it; biological systems resist change, and the rules of their portal won’t allow direct edits to living organisms. Instead, he and Coda keep leaping back, each trying to publish first, each iteration earlier than the last. They’ve both lost count.

Einstein’s image steadies. Infinity, he says, doesn’t care about exhaustion. The two men have built a perfect prison: twin wheels spun by pride. Meanwhile, in Coda’s lab, Hawking’s voice turns grave. The timeline is degrading. Each jump is a stretch; causality is elastic, not unbreakable. Keep pulling, and it snaps. If they continue, they’ll either strand themselves or fracture history so badly that competing versions will coexist and collapse.

Coda’s confidence, thin as glass, cracks. Hawking gives him the only novel move left: stop.

Einstein presses Malik toward the same conclusion. After all these jumps, has Malik tried something radical—simply talking to Coda in the present? Malik starts to protest, then falters. Somewhere between sabotage and preemption, they forgot they still live in the same moment.

Coda prepares another jump to outrun Malik by three days when the lab door bangs open. Malik stands there, equally wrecked by sleeplessness and obsession. Don’t jump, he pleads. Not to win—to survive. He’s measured the quantum fractures; they’re multiplying exponentially. Three or four more jumps and causality collapses. Coda suspects a trick until the data on Malik’s tablet forces his mind into its old, better habits: verify, model, confirm. It’s true. Horrifyingly true.

Their holographic advisors appear side by side, faintly amused that it took so long. Coda and Malik look at each other—once friends, then rivals, now architects of a looming disaster. Malik apologizes first, voice breaking. He let ego run the experiment. Coda meets him halfway. He admits he drove Malik away by hoarding credit and dismissing his theories. They shake hands.

The green orbs pulse in sync, filling the room with emerald light as the timeline begins to knit. Hawking and Einstein fade like satisfied teachers. Equations, bright as breath, write themselves across the lab wall—an unexpected gift: a complete, unified theory of temporal mechanics. Not a prize wrestled from a rival, but a truth revealed to collaborators.

They publish one paper with two names. Headlines follow, not about victory but about science done right. In countless branches the two men make countless choices, but in this version—anchored like a fixed star—the moment of surrender becomes the only way to win.
